coredata.dc:title: Orange-red, green, and blue fluorescence carbon dots for white light emitting diodes
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Ling
Zhang, Li
Yang, Lei
Wu, Xingrong
Zhang, Cheng
Wei, Kang
He, Lifang
Han, Xinya
Qiao, Hongbin
Asiri, Abdullah M.
Alamry, Khalid A.
Zhang, Kui
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are admirable nanomaterials due to their superior optical properties and low cost. However, regulation of the emission colors in CDs for lighting device still remains a challenge. In this work, we developed a green hydrothermal method to obtain three emission colors of CDs using l-tyrosine (for blue CDs), o-phenylendiamine (for green CDs) and l-tyrosine/o-phenylendiamine mixture (for orange-red CDs). These CDs possess good water dispersibility, strong emission with high quantum yields, and excellent photostability. Furthermore, the resulting CDs were dispersed in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix to yield solid state films, where the self-quenching effect in solid state was effectively avoided. A full-color light-emitting diode was fabricated by packing the CDs-PVA composites on the top of a UV-chip, which have superior potential applications in CDs-based solid-state lighting system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Photoluminescene
Carbon dots
Self-quenching effect
Light-emitting diode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of self-quenching-resistance with CDs/PVA composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of formation process of orange-red emissive CDs.
TEM and HRTEM images of B-CDs (a), G-CDs (b) and O-CDs (c); (d-f) Particle size distribution histogram of B- CDs (d), G-CDs (e) and O-CDs (f).
FT-IR spectra of pure l-tyrosine, o-phenylendiamine and three types of CDs.
High-resolution XPS C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra of B-CDs (a), G-CDs (b) and O-CDs (c).
UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra of B-CDs (a), G-CDs (b) and O-CDs (c). The insets are the photos of CDs in ethanol under a 365 nm illumination.
Schematic of self-quenching-resistance mechanism by embedding CDs into PVA matrix.
Optical photographs of CDs in the solid state and CDs/PVA films under 365 nm excitation light.
UV-stability of CDs/PVA films under continuous irradiation with UV light for 8 h.

LED prototypes, based on B-CDs, G-CDs, O-CDs and their mixture, with blue (a), green (b), orange-red (c) and white light (d). Fluorescent images (I), CIE chromaticity coordinates (II), and PL (III) emission spectra of LEDs.
Atomic ratio (%) of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs.
Excitation and emission wavelength, quantum yields from B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs.

head.ce:title: Orange-red, green, and blue fluorescence carbon dots for white light emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah 21589, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are admirable nanomaterials due to their superior optical properties and low cost. However, regulation of the emission colors in CDs for lighting device still remains a challenge. In this work, we developed a green hydrothermal method to obtain three emission colors of CDs using l-tyrosine (for blue CDs), o-phenylendiamine (for green CDs) and l-tyrosine/o-phenylendiamine mixture (for orange-red CDs). These CDs possess good water dispersibility, strong emission with high quantum yields, and excellent photostability. Furthermore, the resulting CDs were dispersed in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix to yield solid state films, where the self-quenching effect in solid state was effectively avoided. A full-color light-emitting diode was fabricated by packing the CDs-PVA composites on the top of a UV-chip, which have superior potential applications in CDs-based solid-state lighting system.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of emerging carbon-based fluorescent materials with at least one dimension less than 10 nm in size [1]. The CDs are mainly composed of sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon atoms, and some other elements (e.g. hydrogen, oxygen and nitrogen) may exist on the edge [2]. The first synthesis of CDs was reported by Xu et al. in 2004, where CDs were prepared via electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments [3]. Since then, CDs have received tremendous research interest because of their outstanding merits, including superior optical properties, high photostability, low cost, and ease of synthesis [4]. The low toxicity and good biocompatibility, in particular, make CDs represent a benign alternative to conventional heavy metal-based semiconductor quantum dots. To date, CDs have been found useful in many applications [5–10], such as optoelectronic devices [4,11–22], bioimaging [23,24], security [25], sensors [26–29], and photocatalysis [30]. Despite the rapid development of CDs in the past two decades, the emission colors of most previously reported CDs were limited to blue or green (i.e., short-wavelength emission), which restrained the range of their practical applications [31–39]. Therefore, the development of facile methods to prepare water-soluble CDs with long-wavelength emission remains a vibrant research area.

Top-down and bottom-up methods are commonly employed to synthesize CDs. Using top-down approaches, the CDs are formed or “broken off” from bulky carbon sources such as graphite [40], carbon nanotubes [41], or carbon fibers [42]. Much larger number of studies are focused on bottom-up synthetic routes, by which utilize almost any imaginable organic precursors, such as amino acids [43,44], organic acids [45], or carbohydrate [32]. In particular, amino acids, as the basic building units of proteins, represent the second most abundant component of human cells, muscles and other tissues [44]. Since they possess both amino and carboxyl groups, amino acids are readily dehydrate via forming amide groups. Under hydrothermal conditions, amino acids will further carbonize, yielding CDs [46]. Therefore, amino acids can be considered as one of perfect molecular precursors for the bottom-up construction of CDs. Numerous studies have reported the preparation of CDs using various amino acids, such as glycine [47], l-tryptophan [43], cysteine [44], and l-serine [48]. However, the emissions of these amino acid-based CDs were generally limited to blue or green colors. In order to achieve tunable emissions, surface modifications that alter the chemical and electronic characteristics of CDs are commonly employed [4,49,50]. By introducing new emissive groups, surface modification was able to create or change the surface states of CDs, thereby resulting in long-wavelength emissions via electron and/or energy transfer [32]. For example, Xiong’s group reported the preparation of full-color emissive CDs from p-phenylenediamine and urea as precursors. By tuning the surface state, the CDs emitted luminescence from blue to red [51]. Sun and co-workers synthesized multi-color emissive CDs using citric acid and urea as precursors. By tuning the amount of surface functional groups (i.e., carboxylic acids), emission colors were altered from blue to green, and to red [4].

Herein, the preparation of highly fluorescent orange-red emissive CDs based on l-tyrosine and o-phenylendiamine was first reported. The carbon nanoparticle surface state could be changed through amidation reactions of carboxylic acid moieties with amino molecules [52]. Thus, three emission colors of CDs were obtained by l-tyrosine (blue CDs), o-phenylendiamine (green CDs) and l-tyrosine/o-phenylendiamine mixture (orange-red CDs), respectively. The relative photoluminescence QYs of the CDs with blue, green, and orange-red emission reached up to 8.6%, 12.6%, and 20.9%, respectively. However, these CDs inevitably aggregated in the solid state, which resulted in undesired self-quenching because of extra non-radiative energy transfer among CDs particle or direct π-π interactions between organic molecules [6]. This drawback highly limits the use of these CDs in the optoelectronic devices. To overcome this issue, the resultant CDs were dispersed in the polymer matrix to retain the fluorescence. Moreover, by mixing controlled amount of blue, green, and orange-red CDs into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), we realized full-color light emitting CDs/PVA composites under 370 nm excitation and developed its application in the field of light-emitting diodes (LEDs).

L-tyrosine, o-phenylendiamine, quinine sulfate, rhodamine 6 G and rhodamine B were all purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). H2SO4, ethanol absolute, ethyl acetate and poly(vinyl alcohol) (M w = 1750 ± 50) were offered from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used as received without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout this work.

The structural properties of the three CDs were characterized using a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL Ltd., Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The TEM samples were prepared by sonicating CDs ethanol solutions and depositing a few drops of the solutions onto a carbon-coated copper grid, followed by drying at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were taken on a Themo-VV ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with mono X-ray source Al Kα excitation. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were measured by a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer in the form of KBr pellets. Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were acquired in a TU-1810 UV–vis spectrophotometer. Photoluminescene (PL) spectra were recorded by a PE LS-55 luminescence spectrometer using an Xe lamp as the excitation source. A spectra scan HAAS-2000 spectroradiometer was applied to analyze Internationale de L’Eclairage (CIE) coordinates and the emission of the LEDs.

The three CDs were synthesized using a simple green route of hydrothermal method. Firstly, l-tyrosine (0.335 g), o-phenylendiamine (0.2 g), or their mixture were dissolved in 50 mL ethanol/water (volume ratio = 1:1) to form a transparent solution. Then, the solution was transferred into a 100 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclaves. After heating at 180 ℃ in oven for 18 h and cooling down to room temperature naturally, the obtained solution was purified via silica column chromatography using a mixture of ethyl acetate and ethanol as the eluent. Afterwards, the powders of three CDs with blue, green and orange-red fluorescence were obtained by freeze-drying and stored for further use.

For the preparation of CDs/PVA-based fluorescent films, 1 g of PVA was dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water, and 3 mg of CDs were dissolved in 5 mL of ethanol/water solution. Afterwards, 1.5 mL of the PVA solution and 500 μL of CDs solution were combined together and stirred until forming a homogeneous mixture, followed by putting into a mold and curing for 24 h at room temperature. Finally, the blue, green, and orange-red emissive CDs/PVA films were obtained using different CDs solutions, respectively. For the white CDs/PVA films, 50 μg of blue CDs, 100 μg of green CDs and 150 μg orange-red CDs were mixed with 500 μL ethanol/water solution, and then 1.5 mL of the PVA was added into the CDs mixture with stirring. After curing of 24 h, the white-light CDs/PVA film was obtained.

InGaN LED chips with central emission of 370 nm were purchased Mingkong photoelectric technology Co. Ltd. The operating voltage of the LED was 5.0 V. A full color/white LED was fabricated by using the CDs/PVA film as luminescent materials and InGaN chip as LED base. When coating the film on the LED, blue, green, orange-red and white area light source were obtained.

The QYs (Φ) of the obtained three CDs were calculated according to a previously reported method [10,53]. Specially, quinine sulfate (QY = 55% in 0.1 M H2SO4), rhodamine 6 G (QY = 95% in ethanol), and rhodamine B (QY = 56% in ethanol) were chosen as the references for the emission range of 400−480 nm (blue CDs), 480−560 nm (green CDs), and 580−610 nm (orange-red CDs), respectively. The QY of the CDs was calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)
∅ T
=
∅ R
(
G T
G R
) (
η T 2
η R 2
)
where
∅ T
is the QY of the testing CDs,
∅ R
is the QY of referenced fluorescene dyes.
G T
and
G R
are the slope from the plot of the integral emission intensity against absorbance at the excitation wavelength of the testing CDs and referenced fluorescene dyes, respectively. η is the refractive index of the solvent. To obtain more reliable results, a series of solutions of CDs and referenced fluorescence dyes were prepared with concentrations adjusted such that the optical absorbance values were between 0−0.1 at desired excitation wavelength.

The CDs were prepared from the precursors comprising of l-tyrosine and o-phenylendiamine using a hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 ). The precursors were first dehydrated to form amide groups, followed by carbonization to yield CDs. To optimize the reaction conditions, the mass ratio between l-tyrosine and o-phenylendiamine was adjusted in parallel reactions, and a ratio of 1:1 was found to yield CDs with the maximum fluorescence emission intensity (Fig. S1 in Supporting information). Similarly, the optimal temperature and reaction time were determined to be 180 ℃ (Fig. S2 in Supporting Information) and 18 h (Fig. S3 in Supporting Information), respectively. In addition, CDs were obtained from pure l-tyrosine and o-phenylendiamine, respectively under the same conditions. Afterwards, the resulting three kinds of CDs named B-CDs (blue emission from l-tyrosine), G-CDs (green emission from o-phenylendiamine) and O-CDs (orange-red emission from l-tyrosine and o-phenylendiamine), respectively.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to characterize the morphology of the three CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 (a–c), all the CDs were spherical and well dispersed. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images provided in the insets confirmed the high crystallinity of these three CDs. All the CDs exhibited nearly identical well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, which is consistent with the d-spacing of the graphene (100) planes [50,51]. The particle size distributions of these CDs were summarized in Fig. 2(d–f). All of the CDs were monodisperse, and the average particle sizes of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs were calculated to be 5.1, 5.9, and 4.4 nm, respectively.

By comparing the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of l-tyrosine, o-phenylendiamine, and the three CDs (Fig. 3 ), the characteristic absorption peaks (red stars mark) associated with l-tyrosine and o-phenylendiamine disappeared after dehydration and carbonization reactions under hydrothermal conditions. The successful chemical functionalization and formation of amide groups on O-CDs surface were confirmed by the appearance of typical peaks around 1700, 1580 and 1400 cm−1, which were assigned to the bending vibration of amide I (CO stretching vibration), amide II (in-plane NH bending) and the vibration of the amide III (CN in amide bonds), respectively [54,55]. Moreover, the three CDs possessed abundant oxygen-containing groups, including hydroxyl OH (3400 cm−1), CO (1230 cm−1) on their surfaces, which make the CDs soluble in water [56]. Vibrations of NH (3280 cm−1), CH (2920 cm−1), CO (1720 cm−1) CC (1520 cm−1) and CN (1426 cm−1) bonds were also observed for the three CDs.

To further investigate the surface composition of the functional CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed. All the three CDs exhibited strong peaks at 284.0, 400.0, and 530.6 eV, attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively (Fig. S4 in Supporting Information). These results indicated that these samples were composed of the same elements. The main structures in the high-resolution spectra were analyzed and reproduced at the background of Shirley (Fig. 4 ), the C 1s spectrum can be divided into four peaks at 284.7, 285.4, 286.2 and 288.9 eV, which are attributed to CC, CN, CO and C＝O bonds, respectively. The N 1s spectrum can be divided into three peaks at 399.0, 400.0 and 400.9 eV, indicating the existence of pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and NH bond, respectively. The O 1s spectrum contains two peaks at 532.1 and 533.1 eV for CO and CO, respectively. The peak intensity of NH (O-CDs N 1s) and CO (O-CDs O 1s) increased drastically, which further confirmed the formation of amide groups on O-CDs surface.

To determine the optical properties of CDs, the UV–vis absorption and PL emission measurements were performed. The three CDs displayed similar absorption properties in the UV region of 200−350 nm, but different properties were observed in the lower energy region (Fig. 5 ). Specifically, all the CDs exhibited a characteristic absorption peak at 230 nm and a shoulder peak at ∼280 nm, which were attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC and CN bonds in the aromatic rings, respectively. However, in the lower-energy region, the three spectra exhibited distinct adsorption bands at 350, 430 and 405 nm, indicating that these three CDs possessed different surface states.

The PL properties of the three CDs were also shown in Fig. 5. Under excitation at 365 nm, B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs solution displayed strong blue, green, and orange-red PL, respectively. Of note, the maximum emission wavelength of B-CDs shifted from 418 to 453 nm, when the excitation wavelength changed from 305 to 425 nm (Fig. 5(a)), indicating that B-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent PL behaviors. The introduction of different functional groups on the surface of CDs is the main factor leading to the existence of multiple emission sites/defect sites. The B-CDs was synthesized by l-tyrosine, the amino and carboxyl groups in the CDs lead to more defect sites on the surface. In contrast, the emission peaks of G-CDs stayed at ∼540 nm, when the excitation wavelength changed from 370 to 490 nm (Fig. 5(b)), indicating an excitation independent emission. Similarly, the emission of O-CDs remained at 590 nm, when the excitation wavelength changed from 365 to 485 nm (Fig. 5(c)).

It is generally agreed that the PL properties of CDs are dependent on their surface states, as the surface functional groups are primarily responsible for trapping the excitons under excitation. The radiative recombination of those surface-trapped excitons would determine the specific PL properties of CDs [57]. Meanwhile, the oxidation degrees of O-CDs surface (Table 1 ) increased with the red shift of the PL. Consequently, CDs with uniform surface properties generally remained the same PL properties even under different excitation energy [58,59]. Therefore, the excitation-independent emission of O-CDs suggested that these CDs might possess uniform surface states.

The PL emission of each CDs clearly originated from the absorption in the lower-energy region. Specifically, the PL quantum yields (QYs) of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs were 8.6%, 12.6% and 20.9%, which were determined using quinine sulfate, rhodamine 6 G, and rhodamine B standards, respectively (Table 2 ). Consistent with above results, the incoporation of o-phenylendiamine into l-tyrosine systems not only resulted in red-shifted fluorescence, but also improved the QYs of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of self-quenching-resistance with CDs/PVA composites

ce:section.ce:para: Once applied into LED applications, CDs-based films will be frequently exposed to UV light for a long period of time, which requires these films possess outstanding photostability. Therefore, the UV-stability of the CDs/PVA films was evaluated by continuously illuminating with UV light for 8 h. As shown in Fig. 8 , the PL intensity of all CDs/PVA films exhibited minimal attenuation over the course of exposure, demonstrating their excellent UV-stability. These data also suggested that the obtained CDs should be stable in the PVA matrix and subjective to aggregation.

As the CDs/PVA films exhibited excellent photostability and good film-forming ability, we employed these films as a light converter for LED devices. Specifically, the devices were fabricated by coating CDs/PVA films on the UV excitation light emitting chips. We have selected LED chips with wavelength of 370 nm to excite all the three CDs effectively, which is very important for white light LED device. As seen in Fig. 9 (I), the devices based on B-CDs/PVA, G-CDs/PVA, and O-CDs/PVA films provided bright blue, green and orange-red illumination, respectively. The Commission Internationale de L’Eclairage 1931 (CIE) chromaticity coordinates of CDs/PVA LEDs were calculated from the emission curve. The CIE coordinates were realized at (0.23, 0.26), (0.34, 0.43), and (0.41, 0.42) for B-CDs/PVA, G-CDs/PVA and O-CDs/PVA films, respectively (Fig. 9(Ⅱ)). The emission spectra of LED devices were consistent with those of corresponding CDs solution, further confirming that the CDs were well dispersed into PVA matrix.

A white-light solid LED was prepared by combining the B-CDs, G-CDs and O-CDs at a weight ratio of 1:2:3 into one PVA films, followed by coating onto a UV-chip (370 nm). The resulting white-light was significant under naked-eye observation, and the CIE color coordinate of white LED was (0.30, 0.33), which was highly adjacent to the coordinate of pure white light (0.33, 0.33). In addition, the correlated color temperature (CCT) and the color rendering index of the white lighting solid LED was 6293 K and 83, respectively, which were associated with cool white light. The external quantum efficiency of the white-light LED was 3.60%. Moreover, the results of the LED working at different driven currents revealed the stability of color output (Fig. S6 in Supporting Information). These results demonstrated that the fabricated white LED met the luminous requirement and should be applicable to both indoor and outdoor lighting system [63]. Compared with recent research on white LEDs (CCT, 5606 K) [64], the present LED had a greater CCT value because of a higher proportion of blue and green light. The emitting light spectrum for this white LED was shown in Fig. 9(III), which covered nearly entire visible-light region (i.e., 380−750 nm). Collectively, these CDs-based white lighting solid LED showed great promise in the field of optoelectronic devices.

In summary, we reported a novel method to prepare CDs with tunable emission colors by changing the molar ratio of suitable carbon sources. Under hydrothermal condition, the as-synthesized CDs exhibited blue and green emission using l-tyrosine and o-phenylendiamine as precursors, respectively. In addition, the orange-red emissive CDs were obtained at the molar ratio of 1:1 (l-tyrosine: o-phenylendiamine). The deleterious self-quenching effect of the CDs was minimized by dispersing these CDs into PVA films. The resulting CDs/PVA films exhibited the bright emission light under excitation. By combining CDs of three colors into single PVA film, white light emitting LED was achieved, suggesting that the developed CDs could strengthen the photoelectric applications of carbon-based materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was finically supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2017YFE0118200), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 61671019, 51703002, and 51474003), the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province, China (No. 1908085MB35), the Natural Science Foundation for the Higher Education Institutions of Anhui Province of China (Nos. KJ2018A0040 and KJ2018A0067), and the Key Lab of Guang Dong Province for High Property and Functional Polymer Materials (No. 20170003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jmst.2020.03.020.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
